ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report
Off Calendar Nov-14-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: The following matters were heard together:
1) PES-17-301322, Estate of Marilyn Mallen; and
2) PTR-18-302018, Marilyn Mallen Trust.

Appearances:
Regina Wang for petitioner and administrator Daniel Mallen, who was also present.
Emilie Calhoun, petitioner and former attorney for Daniel Mallen.
John Boessenecker for co-trustees Kelli Mallen, Mary Mallen, and Kevin Mallen, who were also present.

The matter was called for hearing on 1) First and Final Accounting and Report of Administrator, filed by Daniel Mallen; and 2) Petition for Construction of Trust Terms re: Payment of Probate Expenses, filed by Emilie Calhoun.

The Court confirmed review of the filed papers. Ms. Wang stated she had nothing further to add. Ms. Calhoun highlighted unresolved issues from her opposition, and pointed out inconsistencies in the estate inventory, unexplained removal of assets, and lack of clarity on how the settlement benefitted the estate. She also stated that the dismissal was ambiguous and noted that the settlement documents were executed by Daniel Mallen individually rather than as administrator.

The Court asked Mr. Boessenecker how they would get around Probate Code Section 19001. Mr. Boessenecker argued that the statute could not override the trust's express terms and cited Haggerty v. Barton (2024).

Following discussion, the Court took the matters under submission.

Judge: Stephen M. Murphy; Clerk: Kenneth B. Hunt; Court reporter: Maya Morohoshi, CSR 564, maya.morohoshi5@gmail.com (D204)

Emilie Calhoun, objector and former attorney to Daniel Mallen.
John Boessenecker for and with Kelli Mallen, Mary Mallen, and Kevin Mallen (via Zoom).

Counsel confirmed they had reviewed the tentative ruling. The Court inquired whether the parties were in a position to resolve the dispute or if litigation would be necessary. Ms. Calhoun argued that the Court should approve her attorney's fees and cost reimbursements, asserting that they qualify as administrative expenses. She stated that the estate lacks funds to pay these expenses and that Mr. Mallon, as administrator, would need to determine whether the trust would pay or if she would have to pursue a claim against him personally. She also raised concerns regarding the handling of estate assets, including an alleged unaccounted-for property in Baltimore.

Mr. Hollenberg responded that the estate lacks the necessary funds to pay the claimed fees and questioned Ms. Calhoun-Coper's legal basis for seeking reimbursement.

Mr. Boessenecker stated that the trust does not allow for payment of probate costs and fees except in tax-related matters. He also argued that the Baltimore property was never part of the estate and that any claims regarding it had been previously resolved.

The Court noted that issues regarding the severance of joint tenancy, asset ownership, and attorney's fees remain contested and may require further litigation if not resolved through discussion. The court denied Ms. Calhoun's request to place her fee petition back on calendar and directed her to refile the petition. Ms. Calhoun was also directed to provide notice of her objection to the accounting to the surety, as required by Probate Code Section 1213(a)(2).

The court continued the matter to May 6, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. in Department 204, to allow time to resolve outstanding issues.

The matters are called. Brief discussions regarding the demurrer are held. The Court stated that it would allow the extension of time, that it finds good cause for the additional time, and that once it has the demurrer or motion to strike on file it would go from there/proceed. Kelli Mallen stated that she would only be filing the demurrer, that she doesn't have the bandwith to file the motion to strike, and that she will be focusing on the demurrer. Discussions regarding the bond and 384 Delaware are held. Mr. Boesnnecker stated that he has no problem with the bond being discharged or exonerated. Mary Mallen noted that the property is titled in her name. The Court stated that it wants to save the estate money, waived notice, and exonerated the bond. Ms. Calhoun is to prepare the order exonerating the bond. The Court noted that it was being flexible and has a desire to keep the expenses down. Over Ms. Calhoun's objections the Court extended the time to file the demurrer to January 17, 2023. The Court continued the Status on Close of the Estate to June 26, 2023, at 9:00 a.m. in Dept. 204. Ms. Calhoun presented argument regarding the amount of the distributions, her email requests, and the lack of a meet and confer. Ms. Calhoun stated that there has been stonewalling, that there was a failure to account going on for five years, and requested that the Court bring the trust under court supervision. Mr. Boessnecker responded stating that he did provide information on the distributions, that $410,000.00 is held in reserve, that there was no requirement to meet and confer, and that there is pending litigation. Ms. Calhoun presented extensive argument in support of the Court taking the trust under court supervision. The Court noted that bringing the trust under court supervision is currently before it and that it was not encouraged to do it in the past nor today. Ms. Calhoun presented argument relating to a breach of duties. Mr. Boessenecker responded stating that the issues raised are matters for trial. MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: John Boessenecker appeared by zoom for Petitioners.
Emily Calhoun appeared by zoom for Dan Mallen (Respondent/Administrator).
Mary Mallen also present by zoom.

The court stated that it appears granting the relief sought would require that it find there is some kind of injury to the estate. Mr. Boessenecker stated that at a minimum the property (Baltimore Way) needs to be struck from the inventory and appraisal, that if not, it will cause unnecessary litigation and that there needs to be a quiet title action, or an 850 petition to bring the property into probate. Mr. Boessenecker also stated that it is undisputed that the property is in the name of Mary Mallen, that the problem is that they are going to seek statutory fees and that the property is worth at least two million or more. Ms. Calhoun stated that the estate has an interest of at least 50%, spoke of when deeds were recorded and how title was held at the time of the decedent's death, and asked the court to take judicial notice of grant deeds that were attached as exhibits. Ms. Calhoun also raised the issue of conflict of having Mary as a fiduciary and stated that she wants an evidentiary hearing to determine what percentage of the property ownership belongs to the estate. The Court denied the Petition for Order Terminating Estate and for Discharge of Personal Representative. The court noted that its ruling is not an indication of whether the Baltimore Way property is, or is not an asset of the estate. Ms. Calhoun expressed concern about the trustees selling and liquidating the property, and asked the court to take supervision over the trust. Ms. Calhoun's request is denied.

Mr. Boessenecker reports that he doesn't believe that a bond is required as only approximately $3000 in assets remain.

The court advises counsel that the court is inclined to sign the order regarding the Moncada property and the Order Determining Title to Real Property in the related estate matter is signed in open court. The Accounting Status Petition is continued to July 21, 2011. The Petition for Order Terminating Estate and for Discharge of Personal Representative is continued in open court from July 14, 2021, to July 21, 2021. Ms. Calhoun reports that she intends to file a Petition to Assign a Neutral Trustee and requests permission from the court to schedule it on July 21, 2021. The court grants the request and advises her to add the text "approved in open court" on her petition to advise the filing clerk that she has permission to do so.

Petition For Order Directing Administrator To Administer Estate Notwithstanding Appeal [Probate Code Section 1310(B)]
Off Calendar Sep-25-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Emilie Calhoun for and with Daniel Mallen (Petitioner).
John Boessenecker for and with Kelli Mallen and Mary Mallen (Respondents).

The Court questions why administration of the estate should go on while the appeal is pending, and what is it that is urgent and needs to be done. Ms. Calhoun stated that administration of the estate and protection of the assets needs to be done, that they need to know what the assets are and their value, that no rent is being paid at the Moncada Way property, that assets ($12,500.00 and $25,000.00) were transferred out and that they don't know where they went. Ms. Calhoun also expressed concern about the financial institutions and the title company's practice of purging records, stated that without those records they can not make a determination as to where those monies went, that income tax returns have not been prepared and no one has authority to sign them, that they can not obtain status on the properties without letters and that one of the institutions indicated that they were not told that Ms. Mallen had passed away. In addition, Ms. Calhoun stated that all of the mail is going to the house, that her Client is bonded and the assets will be protected, raised the question of what if the person indicating that they are paying the mortgage and the property taxes stops and stated that declarations are inadmissible because this is a contested matter. Mr. Boessenecker stated that they have had two years to do discovery, that there needs to be clear evidence of eminent injury or loss, that they are raising speculation that his Client will stop making the mortgage payment and that they want letters so that they can file a law suit against his Client to quiet title of the property that was deeded out in 2006. Mr. Boessenecker also that the statute ran out, that the Court can not compel someone to pay rent when living in a house that is in joint tenancy, that he Client tells him that Daniel has copies of documents, and that the issue before the Court of Appeals is who should be the Administrator and whether there is an estate or a trust estate. Mr. Boessenecker also stated that the only asset is the home on Moncada Way, that the only issue is getting records and that they will cooperate with that. Mr. Calhoun stated that transfer of the deed was in 2009, not 2006. Mr. Mallen stated that the loan was secured by his Mother's income and that there is no way to guarantee that the people who are living in the house can keep up with the bills. The Petition for Order Directing Administrator to Administer Estate notwithstanding Appeal is under submission. The Court informed Counsel that it will not sign an order with full authority, stated that either side can provide a form of order indicating what can be done and that the Mr. Boessenecker can provide an order denying the petition.

Judge: Peter J. Busch. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Deputy: C. Kabasares. Not reported.

Off Calendar Jan-15-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Emilie Calhoun and Elina Protich for and with Petitioner Daniel A. Mallen; John Boessenecker for and with Kelli Mallen.

Ms. Protich stated that the decedent owned two properties, one in Baltimore, MD, and the family home on Moncada Way in San Francisco. She stated that the estimated value of the Moncada Street property was between $1,900,000 and $3,000,000, with an encumbrance of $435,000. Mr. Boessenecker replied that the Baltimore property was not part of the estate, and that as contended in the Section 850 petition brought by his client, and currently under submission, the Moncada Street property is part of the trust rather than a probate estate asset. He asserted that there are conflicts among the decedent's offspring which justify the Court's appointment of a neutral, either as trustee under the trust or as administrator of the estate. David Mallen stated that he has no conflict with his siblings, and is willing to waive fees if appointed as representative of the estate. The Court expressed doubt over his ability to appoint a neutral administrator in the absence of a competing petition for probate. Mr. Boessenecker responded that his Section 850 petition should be considered a competing petition sufficient to empower the Court to appoint a neutral. Kelli Mallen informed the Court that the decedent had obtained a loan in the sum of $200,000 against the Moncada Street property back in 2003, and had refinanced that loan to increase the amount to $215,000 in 2006. She stated that two subsequent loans had been more recently obtained to pay off arerearages of state and federal taxes. The matter was taken under submission.

Judge: John K. Stewart. Clerk: Craig Blackstone. Bailiff: Deputy Anasse. (Not reported)
